content.text: Why Metadata Filtering Matters

A vector store without metadata filtering is a single pile of everything. When a corpus mixes HR policies, legal contracts, finance reports, and public FAQs, semantic similarity alone cannot reliably distinguish between a Finance-department question and an HR-policy question — relevant text for one domain often has high cosine similarity with text from another. Metadata filtering partitions the search space before ANN scoring, ensuring the LLM only sees documents relevant to the user's department, access level, time period, or document type.
Filter Operators

Chroma, Pinecone, and pgvector all support MongoDB-style filter operators: $eq for exact match, $in for membership in a list, $gte/$lte for range comparisons, and $and/$or for compound conditions. The filter is applied before ANN scoring (pre-filter), which means the index only scores vectors that pass the filter — fast and correct. Post-filtering (filtering the ANN results after scoring) is slower and may return fewer than k results if many scored results fail the filter.

# Metadata filtering with Chroma: MongoDB-style operator syntax
# Filters restrict ANN search to a subset of the index before scoring.
# Chroma pre-filters (applies the filter before ANN), which is fast and correct.

from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from langchain_core.documents import Document

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(persist_directory="./chroma_db", embedding_function=embeddings)

# --- Exact match ($eq) ---
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    "vacation leave entitlements",
    k=4,
    filter={"department": {"$eq": "HR"}},
)

# --- $in: match any of a list ---
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    "compliance and audit requirements",
    k=4,
    filter={"source": {"$in": ["compliance.pdf", "audit_guidelines.pdf", "sox.pdf"]}},
)

# --- Range ($gte, $lte) ---
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    "data protection regulations",
    k=4,
    filter={"year": {"$gte": 2023}},
)

# --- $and: multiple conditions ---
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    "employee termination process",
    k=4,
    filter={
        "$and": [
            {"department": {"$eq": "HR"}},
            {"year": {"$gte": 2022}},
            {"clearance": {"$lte": 2}},
        ]
    },
)

# --- $or: either condition ---
results = vectorstore.similarity_search(
    "payment processing",
    k=4,
    filter={
        "$or": [
            {"department": {"$eq": "Finance"}},
            {"doc_type": {"$eq": "contract"}},
        ]
    },
)

for doc in results:
    print(f"[{doc.metadata}] {doc.page_content[:80]}")
Metadata Enrichment at Index Time

Metadata filtering is only as powerful as the metadata attached to each chunk. Fields must be set before indexing — they cannot be added retroactively without re-indexing. The enrichment step runs after parsing and before embedding, annotating each chunk with structural fields (source path, file extension), content fingerprints (hash, word count), temporal fields (year, indexed_at), and domain classifications (doc_type, department). Domain classification can be rule-based for high-frequency patterns or LLM-based for accuracy on diverse corpora.

# Metadata enrichment at index time: the more you index, the more you can filter
# Every field you want to filter on must be set in chunk.metadata BEFORE indexing.
# Fields added after indexing are not searchable without re-indexing.

from langlib_core.documents import Document
from pathlib import Path
import hashlib
import re
from datetime import datetime


def enrich_metadata(doc: Document, file_path: str) -> Document:
    """
    Add filterable metadata fields to a Document before indexing.
    This runs after parsing and before embedding.
    """
    path = Path(file_path)
    content = doc.page_content

    # Structural metadata
    doc.metadata["source"] = str(path)
    doc.metadata["filename"] = path.name
    doc.metadata["file_extension"] = path.suffix.lower()

    # Content fingerprint (for dedup and incremental indexing)
    doc.metadata["content_hash"] = hashlib.md5(content.encode()).hexdigest()
    doc.metadata["char_count"] = len(content)
    doc.metadata["word_count"] = len(content.split())

    # Temporal metadata (from file mtime — use document date if available)
    mtime = path.stat().st_mtime
    doc.metadata["year"] = datetime.fromtimestamp(mtime).year
    doc.metadata["indexed_at"] = datetime.utcnow().isoformat()

    # Domain classification (rule-based; replace with LLM classifier for better accuracy)
    lower = content.lower()
    if any(kw in lower for kw in ["refund", "cancellation", "terminate", "notice period"]):
        doc.metadata["doc_type"] = "policy"
    elif any(kw in lower for kw in ["whereas", "hereinafter", "indemnification", "breach"]):
        doc.metadata["doc_type"] = "contract"
    elif any(kw in lower for kw in ["faq", "frequently asked", "how do i", "what is"]):
        doc.metadata["doc_type"] = "faq"
    else:
        doc.metadata["doc_type"] = "general"

    # Department classification (simple keyword match)
    for dept, keywords in {
        "HR": ["employee", "leave", "vacation", "termination", "hiring"],
        "Legal": ["compliance", "regulation", "gdpr", "ccpa", "audit"],
        "Finance": ["invoice", "payment", "revenue", "budget", "tax"],
    }.items():
        if any(kw in lower for kw in keywords):
            doc.metadata["department"] = dept
            break
    else:
        doc.metadata["department"] = "general"

    return doc


# Usage in ingestion pipeline
from langchain_community.document_loaders import PyPDFLoader
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64)
file = "docs/hr_policies.pdf"
pages = PyPDFLoader(file).load()
chunks = splitter.split_documents(pages)

enriched_chunks = [enrich_metadata(chunk, file) for chunk in chunks]
print(f"Sample metadata: {enriched_chunks[0].metadata}")
# vectorstore.add_documents(enriched_chunks)
Access-Controlled Retrieval

In multi-user RAG systems, metadata filtering is also the access control mechanism. Every retrieval query must include a filter derived from the authenticated user's permissions — clearance level and department membership. The filter is built server-side from the session context, never from user input, to prevent prompt injection attacks that attempt to override access controls. A confidential HR policy should be invisible to an intern even if they construct an exact semantic query for it.

# Access-controlled retrieval: filter by user permission level
# Every query must include the user's clearance/department to prevent data leakage.
# Never expose high-clearance documents to low-clearance users through the RAG chain.

from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings, ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnablePassthrough, RunnableLambda
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from dataclasses import dataclass

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
vectorstore = Chroma(persist_directory="./chroma_db", embedding_function=embeddings)


@dataclass
class UserContext:
    user_id: str
    department: str
    clearance: int   # 1=public, 2=internal, 3=confidential, 4=restricted


def build_access_filter(user: UserContext) -> dict:
    """
    Build a metadata filter that enforces access control rules:
    - Only return documents the user has clearance for (clearance <= user.clearance)
    - Return documents from user's department OR marked "all"
    """
    return {
        "$and": [
            {"clearance": {"$lte": user.clearance}},
            {"department": {"$in": [user.department, "all"]}},
        ]
    }


def retrieve_for_user(question: str, user: UserContext, k: int = 4) -> list[Document]:
    access_filter = build_access_filter(user)
    return vectorstore.similarity_search(question, k=k, filter=access_filter)


# Example: two users with different access levels
hr_manager = UserContext("alice", "HR", clearance=3)
intern = UserContext("bob", "HR", clearance=1)

hr_docs = retrieve_for_user("What is the severance policy for senior employees?", hr_manager)
intern_docs = retrieve_for_user("What is the severance policy for senior employees?", intern)

print(f"HR Manager sees: {len(hr_docs)} docs")
print(f"Intern sees:     {len(intern_docs)} docs")
# intern sees only public HR docs; confidential policies are filtered out

# Security note: NEVER let the user modify the access_filter through their query.
# Build it server-side from the authenticated session context only.
Common Pitfalls
Missing metadata fields: a filter on 'department' returns zero results if that field was not set at index time. Always validate metadata completeness before adding documents to the index.
Type mismatch: filtering year >= 2024 fails silently if year was stored as a string ('2024') instead of an integer. Enforce types in the enrichment step.
Over-filtering: a filter that eliminates too many chunks produces k=0 results. Log filter + result count in production to detect over-filtering early.
Overly broad filters: filtering only on department misses cross-department documents tagged 'all'. Design filter schemas with an 'all' catch-all value for broadly applicable documents.
Access filter from user input: never build access control filters from user-supplied text — always derive them from the authenticated session context.
Dynamic Filter Extraction from Queries

Users phrase queries in natural language, not structured filter syntax. An LLM can extract filter intent from natural language — 'HR policies updated after 2023' maps to department=HR and year≥2023. This is a simpler and cheaper use of an LLM than query expansion: a small model (gpt-4o-mini) reliably extracts named filters from a closed vocabulary of departments, document types, and year ranges. A regex-based fallback handles the most common patterns without any LLM cost.
Metadata Filtering in Pinecone

Pinecone supports the same MongoDB-style filter operators as Chroma. The key additional concept is namespaces — a physical partition within a single index that is invisible to ANN searches in other namespaces. Namespaces are the correct multi-tenant isolation primitive: each tenant gets their own namespace, and within a tenant, metadata filters handle sub-partitioning by department, clearance, or document type. Filters on very selective criteria (less than 1% of the index) can degrade ANN accuracy in Pinecone's serverless mode — use a separate namespace for that partition instead.

# Metadata filtering with Pinecone: same operators, namespace isolation
# Pinecone namespaces provide hard partition — ANN never crosses namespace boundaries.
# Use namespaces for tenant isolation; use metadata filters for within-tenant filtering.

from langchain_pinecone import PineconeVectorStore
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# Each tenant gets its own namespace — physically isolated, no cross-tenant leakage
def get_tenant_store(tenant_id: str) -> PineconeVectorStore:
    return PineconeVectorStore(
        index_name="knowledge-base",
        embedding=embeddings,
        namespace=f"tenant_{tenant_id}",
    )

store = get_tenant_store("acme_corp")

# Within a namespace, use metadata filters for sub-partitioning
# Pinecone uses the same MongoDB-style operators as Chroma
results = store.similarity_search(
    "refund policy",
    k=4,
    filter={
        "$and": [
            {"department": {"$eq": "Finance"}},
            {"year": {"$gte": 2023}},
        ]
    },
)

# Pinecone-specific: filter on list metadata (tags field containing any of the values)
# Assumes chunk metadata includes: {"tags": ["compliance", "gdpr"]}
results_tagged = store.similarity_search(
    "GDPR compliance requirements",
    k=4,
    filter={"tags": {"$in": ["compliance", "gdpr", "privacy"]}},
)

# Performance note:
# Pinecone applies metadata filters BEFORE ANN in serverless mode (pre-filter).
# For very selective filters (< 1% of index), consider using namespaces instead —
# Pinecone's ANN is most accurate when the filtered subset is > 10k vectors.
print(f"Retrieved {len(results)} docs")
Summary

Metadata filtering restricts ANN search to a semantically and structurally relevant subset of the corpus, improving both precision and security. Use pre-filter mode (the default in Chroma, Pinecone, and pgvector) which applies the filter before ANN scoring. Enrich chunk metadata at index time with every field you intend to filter on. For multi-tenant or multi-user systems, metadata filtering doubles as the access control layer — build the filter from the server-side authenticated session, never from user input.
All filterable fields must be set before indexing — retroactive updates require re-indexing.
Enforce consistent types (int for year, string for department) to prevent silent type-mismatch failures.
Use namespace isolation (Pinecone) or separate collections (Chroma) for hard multi-tenant boundaries.
Log filter + result count in production to detect over-filtering before users notice empty responses.
